observations.recommendation:
We  recommend  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  direct  the   Inventory
   Committee to undertake the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances  of
   the Municipality following the guidelines and  procedures  prescribed  in
   COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to come up with reliable PPE
   balances  that  are   verifiable   as   to   existence,   condition   and
   accountability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:


        a. Effect the prompt transfer of the cost  of  the  completed  PPEs
           from the TF to the GF books and compute the  depreciation  based
           on the estimated useful life of the assets; and


        b. Take up prior years’ depreciation as Prior Years’ Adjustment  to
           be closed to the Government Equity  account  while  the  current
           year’s depreciation be recorded as expense.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

        a. Review the previous year’s transactions  starting  January  2021
           downward under the Cash in Bank per books with the  transactions
           appearing in the  Bank  Statement,  identify  the  Ᵽ5,583,757.05
           unlocated  difference   in   the   BRS   including   the   check
           disbursements recorded in the  books  but  not  deducted/debited
           from the bank or vice versa;


        b. Ascertain the causes of the  cash  deficit  in  the  Trust  Fund
           books; and


        c. The Municipal officials certifying the  disbursements  ascertain
           that TF disbursements or obligations are in accordance with  the
           trust agreement or approved budget and used  only  for  purposes
           for which the trust funds are intended or  created  pursuant  to
           Section 305, Item E, of  the  Local  Government  Code  of  1991,
           Section 2 of PD No. 1445 and Sections 100 and 101 of the  Manual
           on New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor and/or Municipal Accountant:

        a) Follow up with COA-ICFAO  regarding  the  status  of  previously
           submitted liquidation documents or charges against the  previous
           years’ Confidential Fund;


        b) In case the COA-ICFAO issued  an  Audit  Observation  Memorandum
           (AOM) on the submitted Liquidation  Report,  immediately  inform
           COA-ICFAO that the Accountable Officer was already deceased  and
           compliance of the requirements is not anymore possible; and


        c) Request for the write off of the unliquidated Cash Advances  for
           Confidential Fund of the deceased Municipal Mayor following  the
           provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

        a. Effect the withholding of salaries  from  active  officials  and
           employees who still failed to liquidate their  outstanding  cash
           advances despite final demand letters  had  been  sent  to  them
           pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February
           10, 1997;


        b. Send demand letters or officials and employees who are no longer
           connected with the Municipality or to their dependents in  order
           to recover the amounts due them;


        c. Request for the write off of long outstanding  dormant  advances
           aging 10 years and over following the provisions of COA Circular
           No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and


        d.  Henceforth,  strictly  require  officials  and   employees   to
           liquidate their cash  advances  within  the  prescribed  periods
           pursuant to Item 5.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002  dated  February
           10, 1997.

We recommend that the  Municipal  Mayor  direct  the  OIC-Municipal
   Agriculturist and Municipal Accountant:


        a. To verify the related transactions on  the  acquisition  of  the
           breeding stocks  and  other  bearer  biological  assets  in  the
           previous years; and


        b. Conduct and submit inventory reports indicating  the  prevailing
           market price of each item of biological assets as basis for  the
           taking up of adjustments in the books, pursuant to  Section  4.5
           of COA Circular No. 2016-004 dated      September 30,  2016,  in
           order to reflect reliable balances of Breeding Stocks and  Other
           Bearer Biological Assets accounts in the financial statements.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant take up accrued interest
   payable for accrual of interest on  Loans  Payable-domestic  pursuant  to
   Paragraph 27 of PPSAS I and COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant:

        a. Record all purchase of supplies and materials as inventory while
           issuance to the end-users as expense pursuant to Section114  of
           the Manual on the New Government Accounting System;


        b. Record the expense portion using the  Summary  of  Supplies  and
           Materials Issued (SSMI) together with the original copy  of  the
           Requisition and Issuance Slip (RIS); and


        c. Computation the cost of supplies issued and the ending inventory
           using the moving average method.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant:

        a. Identify  the  reconciling  items  of  reciprocal  accounts  Due
           to/from Other Funds and prepare  necessary  adjustments  in  the
           books in order to present reliable balances of said accounts  in
           the financial statements; and
        b.  Conduct  regular  reconciliation  of  reciprocal  accounts   so
           discrepancies could be easily identified.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRMO to prepare
   the LDRRMFIP annually, indicating the 30% allocation for QRF in lump-sum,
   the allocation for the 70% Mitigation Fund with details  as  to  projects
   and activities to be funded including the list of projects and activities
   charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years pursuant to Item 5.1.2
   of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommend that  the  Municipal  Accountant  submit  the  lacking
   supporting  documents  such  as  the  approved  project  proposal/project
   expenditures, distribution list, and the pictures supporting  the  actual
   distribution  of  the  relief  goods  to  the  affected  individuals  and
   families.
        Henceforth,  the  Municipal  Accountant  must   ensure   that   all
   disbursements are  supported  with  complete  documentation  pursuant  to
   Section 4(6) of PD 1445.

We recommend that the Municipal  Accountant  direct  the  Municipal
   Health Officer and all concerned offices to submit the lacking  documents
   in connection with the payment of COVID-19 AHDP  and  SRA,  such  as  the
   Authority or Office Order and  DTR/Attendance  Records  or  certification
   regarding the number  of  days  that  the  frontline  workers  physically
   reported for work from September 15, 2020 to June 30, 2021,  pursuant  to
   DBM-DOH JC Nos. 1 and 2 dated November 25, 2020  and  DBM-DOH  JC  No.  1
   dated            June 16, 2021.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: Immediately trace the period of acquisition of the PPEs which were not provided with depreciation and compute the depreciation based on the estimated useful life of the assets; Take up prior years’ depreciation as Prior Years’ Adjustment to be closed to the Government Equity account while the current year’s depreciation be recorded as expense; and Draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking up the transfer of the cost of the PPEs recorded under the Trust Fund to the General Fund books and compute the depreciation of these transferred PPEs from the dates they were acquired or available for use and record accordingly the depreciation expense in the books.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant hasten the preparation and submission of monthly BRS of all funds pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 in order to present accurate and reliable Cash-in-Bank, Cash-in-Bank Local Currency Current Account in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and/or Municipal Accountant send a follow up letter and coordinate with COA-ICFAU regarding the status of previously submitted liquidation documents or charges against previous years’ Confidential Fund. Moreover, in case a Notice of Suspension or Disallowance is issued, immediately comply with the requirements in the Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) issued by COA-ICFAU so that said office could correspondingly issue a credit advice or credit notice.
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were prepared and addressed to the previous Municipal Mayor, but the Accounting Department was unable to let those letters duly received by him as no one will receive it. Collection efforts made were proven to be futile. However, as discussed by the current Municipal Accountant with the previous OIC of the Municipal Accounting Office, the latter recalled that some liquidations were sent to COA-ICFAU but they have not booked the liquidation. The previous OIC was advised to locate the files proving that said liquidations were sent to COA-ICFAU so that the current Municipal Accountant as the basis to coordinate with COA-ICFAU.
observations.reason: The amount of ₱4,452,200.00 that remained unliquidated per books as at year ending December 31, 2021 pertains to the confidential fund released to the previous Municipal Mayor sometime in 2015. Unfortunately, according to news, in early December 2021, the said former mayor passed away due to COVID-19 complications. Since the files that could prove that said liquidations were indeed submitted to COA-ICFAU could not be located anymore, the Municipal Accountant has no basis to follow-up COA-ICFAU’s credit advice/credit notice.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: Immediately send final demand letters to active officials and employees who have long outstanding cash advances to settle the overdue amounts within a specific period. Otherwise, effect the withholding of their salaries pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 until such time that the cash advances are fully settled; For officials and employees who are no longer connected with Municipality, send demand letters to them or their dependents in order to recover the amounts due them; Request for the write off of long outstanding dormant advances aging 10 years and over following the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and Henceforth, strictly require officials and employees to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed periods pursuant to Item 5.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
observations.managementAction: Final demand letters have been sent on July and December 2021. The Municipal Accountant has recommended to the Municipal Mayor to call for a meeting with concerned active officials and employees and come up with the amount to be deducted from their respective payroll.
observations.reason:
Request for authority to write-off dormant/long outstanding unliquidated advances aging 10 years and over is yet to be finalized and hopefully be made on the 2nd quarter of 2022.
No more cash advance was granted for officials/employees with pending unliquidated cash advance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: The Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical inventory count of supplies and materials and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semester, respectively; and The Municipal Accountant to take up adjustment in the Inventory Accounts based on the RPCI to reflect the correct cost of supplies and materials in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant transfer the unutilized DRRMF balances to Special Trust Fund by drawing the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) at the end of every calendar year as follows: General Fund: Dr. Subsidy to Other Funds Cr. Cash Trust Fund: Dr. Cash Cr. Trust Liabilities – DRRMF

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that procurement through Negotiated Procurement (Emergency Cases) is completely supported with documentary requirements such as the Omnibus Sworn Statement and Mayor’s/Business Permit if the ABC is ₱500,000.00 or less and additional submission of Income/Business Tax Return if ABC is more than ₱500,000.00, pursuant to the requirement of Item 4.1.5 of GPPB Circular No 04-2016 dated December 20, 2016 as amended by GPPB 05-2020 dated March 20, 2020.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: Direct the BAC to follow the rules and regulations on procurement as provided under RA No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations and the GPPB to obtain the most advantageous price for the government; and Instruct the Municipal Accountant to discontinue the practice of paying huge amounts of disbursements by cash and adhere to the general principle that all disbursements must be paid by check pursuant to the General Principle No. 2 prescribed under COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 9 of COA Circular No. 92-382 dated July 3, 1992 to protect government funds against unauthorized use or misappropriation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: Require the Special Disbursing Officers to submit the lacking liquidation documents such as the Report of Disbursement with supporting documents such as the receipts and invoices relative to their cash advances on food assistance during CY 2020 as required by Item 5.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that Liquidation Reports are supported with complete documentation before recording the liquidation in the books; and Do not allow the transfer of custody of cash advance from one Accountable Officer to another in consonance with Item 4.1.6 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that personnel of the MDRRMO are permanently appointed as mandated under Section 12 of R.A. No. 10121 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 of the NDRRMC, DILG, DBM and CSC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality Government: Intensify the “no segregation, no collection policy” among the residents of the Municipality. Enforce strict implementation of the provisions of Sections 21 and 22 of RA 9003 on the mandatory segregation of solid wastes and carry out the following: Strengthen Information and Education Campaign activities like information dissemination relative to solid waste management. Establish close coordination with barangay officials to ensure that efforts to improve the SWM will reach the constituents at the barangay level. Develop a strategic scheme, like providing incentives, for the purpose of encouraging constituents or enterprises to develop or undertake responsible and effective solid waste management. Impose fines and penalties for any offense made or violation of Section 48 of RA 9003. Provide collection and transport schedules and require the use of separate trucks for specific types of wastes as required by Section 24 of RA 9003. Ensure the establishment or maintenance of MRF in all barangays pursuant to Section 32 of RA 9003. Implement the closure of the open dump, construct and operate a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site for solid wastes to ensure protection of public health and the environment.
observations.reason:
Barangay officials are involved but not all constituents practice mandatory waste segregation at source.
Some barangays do not also maintain MRFs and the Municipality has still not established its own sanitary landfill after the closure of its dumpsite.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Management instruct the concerned officials to insure all properties of the Municipality with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS, so that in case of loss or destruction, the Municipality can at least recover the costs of these assets.
observations.managementAction: All heavy equipment recently acquired were all covered with GSIS Insurance. The completed new Public Market (P140.0million) was already insured (Dec. 1, 2021 to November 30, 2022 coverage).
observations.reason: Insurance coverage for previously constructed buildings was not yet considered.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal  Mayor  direct  the  Inventory
Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory count  of  all  its
properties, plant and equipment and submit the required RPCPPE to  the
Auditor’s Office as provided under Section 124 of the MNGAS for  LGUs,
Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:


a. Immediately trace the period of acquisition of the PPEs which
   were  not  provided  with  depreciation;  and   compute   the
   depreciation based  on  the  estimated  useful  life  of  the
   assets;


b. Take up prior years’ depreciation as Prior Years’  Adjustment
   to be closed to  the  Government  Equity  account  while  the
   current year’s depreciation be recorded as expense.


c. Draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking the transfer of the
   cost of the PPEs recorded under the Trust Fund to the General
   Fund  and  compute  the  accumulated  depreciation  of  these
   transferred PPEs based on the dates the  PPEs  were  acquired
   and record in the books.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant   hasten   the
preparation and submission of monthly BRS pursuant to COA Circular No.
96-011 in order to present accurate and reliable Cash-in-Bank  account
in the financial statements.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  and/or   Municipal
Accountant closely coordinate with COA-ICFAU regarding the  status  of
previously submitted liquidation documents or charges against previous
years’ Confidential Fund. Moreover, in case a Notice of Suspension  or
Disallowance is issued, immediately comply with  the  requirements  in
the Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) issued  by  COA-ICFAU  so  that
said office could correspondingly issue  a  credit  advice  or  credit
notice.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:


a. Require the accountable officers and employees  to  liquidate
   their outstanding cash advances, through  submission  of  the
   necessary supporting  documents  and  refund  the  unexpended
   balance, if any;


b. Immediately  send  final  demand  letters  to  officials  and
   employees who have long outstanding cash advances  to  settle
   the overdue amounts  within  a  specific  period.  Otherwise,
   effect the withholding of their salaries pursuant to 5.1.3 of
   COA Circular  No.  97-002  until  such  time  that  the  cash
   advances are fully settled;


c. Conduct immediate review of accounts that were  inadvertently
   recorded  to  improper  accounts   and   effect   appropriate
   adjustments in the books;


d. Henceforth,  strictly  require  officials  and  employees  to
   liquidate their cash advances within the  prescribed  periods
   pursuant to 5.1 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10,
   1997; and


e. Request  for  the  write  off  of  long  outstanding  dormant
   advances aging 10 years and over following the provisions  of
   COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

submission of liquidation report relative to the  grant  of  financial
assistance to the barangays. Additionally, we recommend that the terms
of reference as to the specific  period  to  liquidate  the  funds  be
indicated in the MOA so that the  Municipality  can  demand  immediate
liquidation from the barangay once the program or projects, for  which
the funds were intended, are fully completed.

comply with the requirement of Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-
002 dated September 12, 2012 regarding the preparation and  submission

sanitary  landfill  following  the  minimum  criteria  provided  under

achieve a safer and organized system or way of waste management.

Other outstanding advances of officers and employees are to be subjected to salary deduction the following year 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the In-charge in the Confidential Fund to comply with the requirements of Item 6.1.8.4 and Item D of 6.3.3 of Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015 on the submission of cash advance vouchers charged to confidential fund together with the supporting documents, within the prescribed period to the COA office.
observations.managementAction: The personnel in-charged of the Confidential Fund has already complied with the submission of vouchers and supporting documents to COA office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant closely monitor the liquidation of all cash advances of the Disbursing Officer and ensure that excess and/or unutilized cash advances, if any, are immediately refunded after the purpose for which the cash advance was granted has been fulfilled. Further, she must ensure that no additional cash advance is granted to any official or employee unless the previous cash advance given to him is first settled or liquidated pursuant with Section 89 of P.D. 1445.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, being the direct supervisor of the Disbursing Officer, ensure that all cash advances granted to him are fully liquidated within the prescribed period.
observations.managementAction:
The concerned disbursing officer has fully refunded the unutilized cash advances under his accountability through cash refund and full deduction from his terminal leave pay.

The Municipal Accountant has made sure that any excess and/or unutilized cash advances were immediately refunded after the purpose for which the cash advance was granted has been fulfilled.  Further ensured that no additional cash advance was granted to anyone with unliquidated previous cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality fast track the implementation of the two BuB projects funded by the DOH to ensure that the desired benefits/services could be delivered to the Municipality’s constituents on time and could address the needs of the poor sectors of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate an active Project Monitoring Committee of Local Poverty Reduction Action Team (LPRAT) or a BuB focal person to monitor the implementation of all BuB projects pursuant to the guidelines under DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 7 dated November 3, 2015.
observations.managementAction: All pending projects were already done.

During the year, the Accounting Dept was doing parallel implementation (GAAMS with manual recording).  The system will be fully implemented in January 2020.

With GAAMS, all cash transactions will be easily extracted and analyzed for bank reconciliation

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator ensure that only transactions/expenses related to programs/projects/activities embodied in DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 shall be charged against the 20% Development Fund in order to achieve or attain the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

For projects that do not partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures but are related to socio- economic development and environmental management, the same can be charged against the General Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended that the City Accountant:

Strictly comply with the documentary requirements for the transfer of funds to barangay as implementing agencies of government program and projects pursuant to Section 4(6) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 and Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

Record fund transfers to barangays under Due from LGUs account instead of Subsidy to Local Government Units for easy monitoring of all fund transfers to barangays and their liquidations.

We recommended that the Municipal Government revive its LCPC and appropriate the budgetary requirement equivalent to 1% of the IRA for LCPC programs, projects and activities in compliance with R.A. No. 9344, otherwise known as the Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act of 2006.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality Government:

Intensify the “no segregation, no collection policy” among the residents of the Municipality.
Enforce strict implementation of the provisions of Sections 21 and 22 of RA 9003 on the mandatory segregation of solid wastes and carry out the following:
Strengthen Information and Education Campaign activities like information dissemination relative to solid waste management.
Establish close coordination with barangay officials to ensure that efforts to improve the SWM will reach the constituents at the barangay level.
Develop a strategic scheme, like providing incentives, for the purpose of encouraging constituents or enterprises to develop or undertake responsible and effective solid waste management.
Impose fines and penalties for any offense made or violation of Section 48 of RA 9003.
Provide collection and transport schedules and require the use of separate trucks for specific types of wastes as required by Section 24 of RA 9003.
Ensure the establishment or maintenance of MRF in all barangays pursuant to Section 32 of RA 9003.

Implement the closure of the open dump, construct and operate a sanitary landfill as a final disposal site for solid wastes to ensure protection of public health and the environment.
observations.reason:
Barangay officials are involved but not all constituents practice mandatory waste segregation at source.

Some barangays do not also maintain MRFs and the Municipality has still not established its own sanitary landfill after the closure of its dumpsite.

observations.recommendation:

We recommended that the School Board refrain from utilizing the SEF fund for payment of expenses that are not priority program, projects and activities in compliance to section 363(c) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of the Local Government Code and DepEd, DBM and DILG JC No.1 s. 2017.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  direct  the  Inventory
   Committee to conduct a complete  physical  inventory  count  of  all  its
   properties, plant and equipment and submit the  required  RPCPPE  to  the
   Auditor’s Office pursuant to the requirement of Section 124 of the  MNGAS
   for LGUs, Volume I and Section 490 of GAAM, Volume I.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  and   the   Municipal
   Accountant:

        a. Strictly require officials and employees to liquidate their cash
           advances within the prescribed period to avoid the  accumulation
           of outstanding balances pursuant to 5.1 of COA Circular No.  97-
           002 dated February 10, 1997;


        b. Issue a demand letter  to  officials  and  employees  especially
           those who are still in active service to  settle  their  overdue
           accounts. Otherwise, effect the withholding of salaries pursuant
           to 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 until  such  time  that  the
           cash advances are fully settled. For employees who are no longer
           connected  with  the  Municipal  Government,  issue   collection
           letters to them or to their relatives to recover the amounts due
           from them;


        c. Send a follow-up letter to COA – ICFAU to ascertain  the  status
           of  previously  submitted  liquidation  documents  for   charges
           against previous years’ confidential fund and  request  issuance
           of credit advice or credit notice; and


        d.  File  request  for  authority  to  write-off  dormant  or  long
           outstanding unliquidated cash advances,  if  necessary,  to  the
           Office of the Auditor pursuant  to  COA  Circular  No.  2016-005
           dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the In-charge in the
   Confidential Fund to comply with the requirements  of  Item  6.1.8.4  and
   Item D of 6.3.3 of Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January  8,  2015  on
   the submission of cash advance  vouchers  charged  to  confidential  fund
   together with the supporting documents, within the prescribed  period  to
   the COA office.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  closely  monitor  the
   liquidation of all cash advances of the  Disbursing  Officer  and  ensure
   that excess and/or unutilized cash  advances,  if  any,  are  immediately
   refunded after the purpose for which the cash  advance  was  granted  has
   been fulfilled. Further, she must ensure that no additional cash  advance
   is granted to any official or employee unless the previous  cash  advance
   given to him is first settled or liquidated pursuant with Section  89  of
   P.D. 1445.


        We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, being the  direct
   supervisor of the Disbursing  Officer,  ensure  that  all  cash  advances
   granted to him are fully liquidated within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipality fast track the  implementation
   of the two BuB projects funded by the DOH  to  ensure  that  the  desired
   benefits/services could be delivered to the  Municipality’s  constituents
   on time  and  could  address  the  needs  of  the  poor  sectors  of  the
   Municipality.


        We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor  designate  an  active
   Project Monitoring Committee  of  Local  Poverty  Reduction  Action  Team
   (LPRAT) or a BuB focal person to monitor the implementation  of  all  BuB
   projects  pursuant  to  the  guidelines  under  DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC  Joint
   Memorandum Circular No. 7 dated November 3, 2015.

We recommended that Municipal Accountant hasten the preparation and
   submission of monthly BRS pursuant to COA Circular No. 96-011 in order to
   present accurate and  reliable  Cash-in-Bank  account  in  the  financial
   statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:


        a.       Record all fund transfers to barangays under Due from LGUs
           account instead of Subsidy to LGUs for easy  monitoring  of  all
           fund transfers to barangays including their liquidations; and


        b. Certify that previous funds transferred has been liquidated  and
           accounted for in the books in compliance with Item 3.1.1 of  COA
           Circular No. 2012-001 dated  June  14,  2012  and  COA  Circular
             No. 2016-002 dated May 31, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the MDRRM Office and
   Council to implement the programs and activities in the LDRRMFIP based on
   timelines and adequately prepare a plan in consonance  with  NDRRMC,  DBM
   and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

        We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the Report
   on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF to COA office monthly  in  compliance
   with Item 5.1.4 of COA Circular 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure  that  personnel  of
   the MDRRMO are permanently appointed as mandated under Section 12 of R.A.
   No. 10121 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 of the  NDRRMC,  DILG,
   DBM and CSC.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the  Municipality  conduct  a  complete  physical
   inventory of all its properties,  plant  and  equipment  and  submit  the
   required RPCPPE to the Auditor’s Office pursuant to  the  requirement  of
   Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I  and  Section  490  of  GAAM,
   Volume I.

We recommend that Municipal  Mayor  direct  the  OIC-Accountant  to
   hasten the preparation and submission of  monthly  BRS  pursuant  to  COA
   Circular No. 96-011 in order to present accurate  and  reliable  Cash-in-
   Bank account in the financial statements.

We recommend that in cases of lump-sum  appropriations  wherein  no
   specific projects or no sufficient  details  were  provided,  a  separate
   Sangguniang Bayan ordinance or resolution must be secured to serve as  an
   authority of the Municipal Mayor to enter into a contract  in  compliance
   with Section 22(c) of R.A. No. 7160 and COA Memorandum No. 2010-014 dated
   April 22, 2010.

We recommend that the Municipal  Mayor  and  the  OIC  –  Municipal
   Accountant:

        a. Strictly require officials and employees to liquidate their cash
           advances within the prescribed period pursuant  to  5.1  of  COA
           Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.


        b.  Issue  a  second  demand  letter  to  officials  and  employees
           especially who are still  in  active  service  to  settle  their
           overdue accounts. Otherwise, effect the withholding of  salaries
           pursuant to 5.1.3 of COA Circular No.  97-002  until  such  time
           that the cash advances are fully settled. For employees who  are
           no  longer  connected  with  the  Municipal  Government,   issue
           collection letters to them or to their relatives to recover  the
           amounts due from them.


        c. Send a follow-up letter to COA – ICFAU to ascertain  the  status
           of  previously  submitted  liquidation  documents  for   charges
           against previous years’ confidential fund and  request  issuance
           of  credit  advice  or  credit  notice.


        d.  File  request  for  authority  to  write-off  dormant  or  long
           outstanding unliquidated cash advances,  if  necessary,  to  the
           Office of the Auditor pursuant  to  COA  Circular  No.  2016-005
           dated December 19, 2016.

We recommend that the City Accountant:

        1. Strictly  comply  with  the  documentary  requirements  for  the
           transfer of funds  to  barangays  as  implementing  agencies  of
           government program and projects  pursuant  to  Section  4(6)  of
           Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 and Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular
           No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.


        2. Record fund transfers to barangays under Due from  LGUs  account
           instead of Subsidy to Local Government Units for easy monitoring
           of all fund transfers to barangays and their liquidations.

We recommend that the municipality comply with the requirements  of
   Item 6.1.8.4 and Item D of 6.3.3 of  Joint  Circular  No.  2015-01  dated
        January  8,  2015  on  the  submission  of  cash  advance  vouchers,
   pertaining  to  confidential  expenses  together  with   the   supporting
   documents, within the prescribed period to the COA office.

We recommend that the Municipal  Government  revive  its  LCPC  and
   appropriate the budgetary requirement equivalent to 1%  of  the  IRA  for
   LCPC programs, projects and activities in compliance with R.A. No.  9344,
   otherwise known as the Juvenile Justice and Welfare Act of 2006.

We recommend that:

           a. The LDRRM Council propose for the revision of the LDRRM  Plan
              and introduce new activities and projects that would  reduce,
              avoid, respond and manage the adverse impacts of disasters in
              order to  achieve  the  country’s  overall  goal  of  “safer,
              adaptive   and   disaster   resilient   communities   towards
              sustainable development”.


           b. The Municipal Accountant prepare and certify  the  Report  on
              Sources and Utilization of DRRMF using the format in Annex  B
              of COA Circular No. 2012-002 and the LDRRM Officer submit the
              said report to COA on or before the 15th day after the end of
              each month to the COA Office.

We reiterate that:        a.  The Management, through its General Services Office, conduct physical inventory of all properties owned by the LGU and prepare the corresponding report to comply with Section 124 of the NGAS Manual and Section 490 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual. It should reconcile the inventory report with the accounting records to present the real and actual cost of the properties. Subsidiary ledgers should be maintained and record PPE’s condition, its whereabouts and other details that are necessary to prove its existence and ownership. 

b. The Management makes the necessary adjustments if there are discrepancies noted between the books and the results of the actual inventory of the accounts to present a reliable balance in the financial statements. Properties with no available historical records should be appraised/revalued by an Appraisal Committee created by the Local Chief Executive.

           present dump site into a sanitary landfill and/or  converting  a
           new technological  approach  to  solid  waste  management  where
           wastes are better managed, controlled and contained.


   Management Comment:


        Recommendations are well taken.

We recommend that management should conduct physical inventory of all properties owned by the LGU and prepare the corresponding report to comply with Section 124 of the NGAS Manual and Section 490 of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual. It should reconcile the inventory report with the accounting records to present the real and actual cost of the properties. Subsidiary ledgers should be maintained and record PPE’s condition, its whereabouts and other details that are necessary to prove its existence and ownership.

We further recommend that management makes the necessary adjustments if there are discrepancies noted between the books and the results of the actual inventory of the accounts to present a reliable balance in the financial statements. Properties with no available historical records should be appraised/revalued by an Appraisal Committee created by the Local Chief Executive.
observations.managementAction: Created an appraisal Committee and conducted a partial inventory on the serviceable and unserviceable properties.
observations.reason: Each Department had submitted to the Office of the Municipal Treasurer the lists of serviceable and unserviceable properties but not yet submitted to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct the officers concerned to see to it that all claims for wages are fully documented before payment is made.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend the strict compliance with the laws, rules and regulations on cash advances that are embodied in the provisions of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-004 dated April 19, 1996 should be adhered to.

We also recommend the withholding of salaries of those officials and employees who failed to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed period until such time that the cash advance is fully settled.

We further recommend that the agency hasten the submission of requirements to the COA Head Office necessary for the issuance of credit notice in order to settle the outstanding cash advances for Confidential and Intelligence Expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the municipality implement the projects funded by other government agencies as required in the Memorandum of Agreement so that the purpose for which these funds were granted are achieved thus, benefitting its constituents.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that Bids and Awards Committee adheres to the provision of R.A. 9184 that the procuring entity abides by the standards set forth by the Act and its IRR and Item 9.2 of COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommend that the practice of specifying the brand names in the Purchase Requests be stopped pursuant to Section 18 of the IRR of R.A. 7190 to get the widest range of bid proposals and the best offer for the government.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the municipality enforce the full implementation of the programs, projects and activities embodied in the annual development plan so that a positive impact on a wider scale can be felt in the locality.
